old_claim.note: Databases (Relational DBMS) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct Search Engines to Relational DBMS edge remains because early web search engines required searchable catalogs or indexes, not the relational database model specifically.

source_shape.source_locator: Architecture discussion describes crawling, repository storage, document indexes, lexicons, hit lists, forward indexes, and inverted indexes for a web search engine.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes specialized search-engine storage and indexing structures rather than a dependency on relational DBMS technology.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports removing the relational-DB prerequisite because the search-engine architecture depends on indexing and retrieval structures that are not equivalent to the relational model.

ontology_before: Search Engines depended directly on relational DBMS technology, implying web search required relational database systems rather than generic indexing/storage.
ontology_after: Search Engines keep web and algorithmic dependencies while database-specific implementations are left out unless a narrower search-storage node is added.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: removed a storage-implementation-specific edge. Preserved: search engines still require web resources and algorithmic indexing/query methods.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to a specific relational-database-backed site-search product.
A source shows first-generation web search engines required relational DBMS engines.
A narrower relational_search_backend node is introduced and intentionally depends on relational DBMS.
short_reason: Search engines need indexes and storage, but not relational DBMS specifically.
